root_cause: Database connection pool was not releasing idle connections, so heap usage grew unbounded until the kernel OOM-killed the container (a slow memory leak).
resolution: Set maxIdleConnections=10 in the Helm values for the pool and added an explicit container memory limit (requests 512Mi / limits 1Gi).
postmortem_summary: PaymentService pods were OOMKilled during peak traffic. Idle DB connections were never reclaimed, so memory climbed steadily until each pod hit its limit and restarted, causing p99 latency spikes. Capping idle connections and setting a memory limit stabilized it.

root_cause: Connection pool exhaustion: a traffic spike opened more concurrent DB connections than the pool max, so new requests blocked waiting for a free connection and timed out.
resolution: Raised pool maxConnections, added a connection acquire-timeout, and enabled PgBouncer in transaction pooling mode.
postmortem_summary: During a flash sale OrdersService exhausted its DB connection pool. Requests queued for connections and timed out, returning 503s. Increasing pool size and fronting Postgres with PgBouncer resolved the contention.

root_cause: The TLS certificate for the public API gateway expired because cert-manager renewal had silently failed for weeks (ACME HTTP-01 challenge blocked by a firewall rule).
resolution: Manually renewed the certificate, fixed the firewall rule blocking the ACME challenge, and added an alert on certificate expiry < 14 days.
postmortem_summary: All external clients lost connectivity when the gateway's TLS cert expired. cert-manager could not complete the ACME challenge due to a firewall change, and no alert existed for pending expiry. A monitor on cert lifetime now prevents recurrence.

root_cause: ArgoCD stopped syncing because a manually-edited live resource created config drift, putting the application into an OutOfSync/Degraded state that halted automated rollout.
resolution: Reverted the manual change, re-enabled auto-sync with self-heal, and disabled kubectl edit on the namespace via RBAC.
postmortem_summary: A hotfix applied directly with kubectl drifted from Git, so ArgoCD refused to sync new deploys and kept serving the old version. Restoring GitOps discipline and enabling self-heal fixed the pipeline.

root_cause: A new deployment added a high-cardinality label (per-request UUID) to a metric, exploding time-series count and exhausting Prometheus memory, which triggered a cascade of false alerts.
resolution: Dropped the offending label via metric_relabel_configs, added cardinality limits, and enforced a metrics review in CI.
postmortem_summary: A per-request UUID label caused a cardinality explosion that OOMed Prometheus and produced an alert storm. Relabeling to drop the label and adding cardinality guards restored monitoring.

root_cause: CoreDNS was overwhelmed after a config change reduced its replica count while cache TTL was set too low, causing excessive upstream lookups and NXDOMAIN spikes under load.
resolution: Scaled CoreDNS replicas, increased cache TTL, and enabled autopath / node-local DNS cache.
postmortem_summary: Reducing CoreDNS replicas plus a low cache TTL flooded upstream DNS, so internal calls intermittently failed to resolve and returned 5xx. Scaling DNS and adding node-local caching stabilized resolution.

root_cause: A verbose debug log left enabled in production filled node disks; the kubelet hit DiskPressure and began evicting pods, cascading load onto remaining nodes.
resolution: Disabled debug logging, added log rotation with size limits, and set node disk-usage alerts plus ephemeral-storage requests/limits.
postmortem_summary: Debug logging accidentally shipped to prod filled node disks, triggering DiskPressure evictions that rippled across the cluster. Rotating logs and adding disk alerts and storage limits prevented recurrence.

root_cause: A slow downstream call exceeded the Kafka max.poll.interval, so the broker repeatedly kicked and rebalanced the consumer group, creating a rebalance storm and reprocessing.
resolution: Increased max.poll.interval.ms, reduced max.poll.records, and moved the slow call to an async worker with idempotent processing.
postmortem_summary: A slow synchronous downstream call blew past Kafka's poll interval, causing continuous consumer rebalances, growing lag, and duplicate work. Tuning poll settings and making processing async + idempotent resolved it.

root_cause: Redis reached maxmemory and the allkeys-lru policy evicted hot session keys, so traffic fell through to the database and overloaded it (a thundering-herd cache stampede).
resolution: Increased Redis memory, switched to volatile-lru with explicit TTLs, and added request coalescing / singleflight on cache misses.
postmortem_summary: Redis hit its memory ceiling and evicted hot keys, causing a cache-miss stampede that overloaded Postgres. Raising memory, using TTL-scoped eviction, and coalescing misses fixed the cascade.

root_cause: A too-aggressive readiness probe (1s timeout, 1 failure threshold) marked healthy pods as not-ready during brief GC pauses, so the ingress removed backends and returned 502s.
resolution: Relaxed readiness probe thresholds/timeouts and tuned JVM GC to reduce pause times.
postmortem_summary: An overly strict readiness probe flapped backends in and out of rotation during GC pauses, producing intermittent 502s at the ingress. Loosening the probe and tuning GC stabilized routing.

root_cause: A newly deployed WAF managed rule contained a regular expression with catastrophic backtracking, which spiked CPU to 100% across the edge fleet and served 502s globally.
resolution: Rolled back the WAF rule via a global kill switch and later added regex complexity checks and a performance test gate for rules.
postmortem_summary: A WAF rule with a pathological regex caused catastrophic backtracking that pinned CPU across Cloudflare's edge, taking sites offline. A global rollback restored service; regex safety checks were added to prevent recurrence.

root_cause: During routine debugging an operator ran a command with a mistyped parameter that removed far more capacity than intended, forcing a restart of core S3 subsystems that took hours to recover.
resolution: Restarted the affected subsystems, and afterward added tooling guardrails to prevent removing capacity below safe minimums and to speed restarts.
postmortem_summary: A typo during maintenance removed too many S3 index/placement servers in us-east-1, requiring a full subsystem restart and causing a multi-hour outage that rippled across the internet. Guardrails were added to block unsafe capacity removal.

root_cause: While mitigating a replication issue at night, an engineer accidentally ran a directory removal against the primary database instead of the secondary, deleting production data; several backup methods were found not to be working.
resolution: Restored from a six-hour-old staging copy, then overhauled backups, added restore testing, and reduced dangerous manual access.
postmortem_summary: During a late-night incident an engineer deleted the primary Postgres data directory by mistake, and most backups were non-functional, forcing recovery from a stale copy with data loss. GitLab rebuilt its backup and restore-testing practices.

root_cause: A latent software bug, introduced earlier, was triggered when a specific customer configuration change was pushed, causing edge nodes worldwide to return errors.
resolution: Disabled the triggering configuration, deployed a fix for the underlying bug, and accelerated the global rollout of the patch.
postmortem_summary: A dormant bug in Fastly's edge software was tripped by a valid customer config change, briefly taking down a large slice of the web. Reverting the config and patching the bug resolved it within about an hour.

root_cause: A brief network partition between data centers triggered an automated MySQL failover that left the East and West coasts with divergent writes, requiring careful reconciliation.
resolution: Paused writes, reconciled the divergent databases, and later revised failover automation to avoid cross-region split-brain.
postmortem_summary: A 43-second network partition caused an automated MySQL topology failover that split writes across regions, degrading GitHub for roughly 24 hours while data was reconciled. Failover automation was hardened afterward.

root_cause: A post-holiday traffic surge coincided with saturation of an AWS Transit Gateway and autoscaling that could not add capacity fast enough, cascading into failures across internal services.
resolution: Scaled the network gateways, adjusted autoscaling to pre-provision, and improved load-shedding and dependency isolation.
postmortem_summary: Returning-from-holiday traffic saturated an AWS Transit Gateway while autoscaling lagged, so Slack could not provision capacity fast enough and cascaded into a broad outage. Pre-provisioning and better load-shedding were introduced.
